enrichment.short_description: SPIP is a free, open-source content management system created in France for building collaborative, multilingual websites without deep technical expertise.
enrichment.seo_title: SPIP: Free Open-Source CMS for Collaborative Publishing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SPIP is a free, open-source French CMS built for collaborative, multilingual website publishing. See features, pricing, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SPIP (Systeme de Publication pour l'Internet Partage) is a free, open-source content management system first released on July 1, 2001 by the minirezo collective in France. It was built to let journalists, associations, and webmasters publish and structure articles collaboratively without needing deep technical skills. SPIP is distributed under the GNU General Public License, so it can be downloaded, self-hosted, modified, and redistributed at no cost for personal, nonprofit, institutional, or commercial sites. The platform is especially popular in the French-speaking web and has been used by organizations such as Le Monde diplomatique for multilingual editorial workflows. SPIP ships with strong multilingual publishing support, covering more than 20 interface languages, and a plugin ecosystem for extending templates, forms, and site structure. There is no vendor company behind SPIP; it is maintained by a volunteer community of developers, documentation writers, and translators who coordinate through open forums and mailing lists. Because it is self-hosted, users are responsible for their own server, so total cost depends on hosting rather than software licensing. SPIP suits users who want an editorially focused, collaborative publishing tool and are comfortable with (or want to avoid) the complexity of larger CMS platforms like WordPress or Drupal.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is SPIP
paragraphs: SPIP is an open-source content management system built specifically around collaborative editorial workflows. Rather than being a general-purpose website builder, it was designed from the start for teams of writers and editors who need to structure, review, and publish articles together, with built-in roles for authors, editors, and administrators.

Since its 2001 launch, SPIP has grown into a mature platform with a large plugin catalog and an active community, primarily in France and the wider French-speaking world, though it is used internationally and translated into more than 20 languages. It remains free under the GNU GPL, with no paid tiers or licensing fees for the core software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: SPIP's core strength is its editorial workflow: articles move through draft, proposed, and published states, with role-based permissions that let editorial teams collaborate without stepping on each other's work. Its templating system, built on a custom language called SPIP loops, separates content from presentation, making it possible to restyle a site without touching the underlying data.

The platform also includes native multilingual publishing tools, so a single site can maintain parallel versions of content in different languages with translation tracking. A large community plugin directory extends SPIP with forums, newsletters, e-commerce, SEO helpers, and custom fields, while its lightweight footprint makes it a practical option for organizations that want a self-hosted CMS without the overhead of larger platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SPIP itself is entirely free to download and use, since it is licensed under the GNU General Public License. There are no editions, tiers, or user limits built into the software.

The only real costs are self-hosting related: web hosting, a domain name, and optionally paid support or development from independent SPIP freelancers and agencies in the community, since there is no official commercial vendor offering paid plans.

features.title: Collaborative editorial workflow
features.description: Role-based publishing states (draft, proposed, published) let writers, editors, and admins collaborate on articles with clear permissions.

features.title: Native multilingual publishing
features.description: Built-in tools for maintaining and tracking translated content across more than 20 languages on a single site.

features.title: Template separation via SPIP loops
features.description: A custom templating language keeps content and design separate, so themes can be changed without touching site data.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: A community-maintained plugin directory extends SPIP with forums, newsletters, SEO tools, custom fields, and more.

features.title: Self-hosted and license-free
features.description: Runs on standard PHP and MySQL hosting with no software licensing fees under the GNU GPL.

features.title: Lightweight footprint
features.description: Designed to run efficiently on modest hosting, making it accessible to small associations and nonprofits.

features.title: Active community governance
features.description: Maintained by volunteer developers, documenters, and translators rather than a single commercial vendor.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing costs
Strong built-in support for multilingual and collaborative publishing
Long track record and mature codebase dating back to 2001
Lightweight and runs well on modest hosting
Active French-language community and extensive plugin catalog
cons: Smaller international community and fewer English-language resources than WordPress or Drupal
Steeper learning curve for its custom templating language
No official commercial support channel or SLA
Plugin quality and maintenance varies since it is entirely community-driven
Less common outside the French-speaking web, so fewer premium themes and integrations

faqs.question: Is SPIP really free
faqs.answer: Yes. SPIP is licensed under the GNU General Public License, so it can be downloaded, installed, and modified at no cost for personal, nonprofit, or commercial use.

faqs.question: Who maintains SPIP
faqs.answer: SPIP is maintained by an open-source community of developers, documentation writers, and translators rather than a single company, coordinated through public forums and mailing lists.

faqs.question: What is SPIP best suited for
faqs.answer: SPIP is best suited for editorial teams, associations, and institutions that need collaborative, multilingual publishing workflows rather than a general-purpose website builder.

faqs.question: Does SPIP support multiple languages
faqs.answer: Yes. SPIP has native multilingual publishing tools and an interface translated into more than 20 languages, making it popular for sites that need parallel-language content.

faqs.question: Can I get paid support for SPIP
faqs.answer: There is no official commercial vendor, but independent freelancers and agencies within the SPIP community offer paid development and support services.

faqs.question: What technology does SPIP run on
faqs.answer: SPIP is a PHP application typically paired with a MySQL database, and it can run on standard shared or managed web hosting.

faqs.question: How does SPIP compare to WordPress
faqs.answer: SPIP is more narrowly focused on collaborative editorial publishing and multilingual sites, while WordPress has a much larger plugin and theme ecosystem and broader general-purpose use.

faqs.question: Is SPIP still actively developed
faqs.answer: Yes. SPIP continues to receive stable releases from its community, with recent versions in the 4.x series.

use_cases.title: Association and nonprofit websites
use_cases.description: Small organizations use SPIP to publish news and articles collaboratively without paying for software licenses.

use_cases.title: Multilingual editorial publishing
use_cases.description: Media outlets and institutions maintain parallel-language content with SPIP's built-in translation tracking.

use_cases.title: Institutional and government sites
use_cases.description: Public sector bodies in French-speaking regions use SPIP for its long track record, GPL licensing, and self-hosting control.

features: Full access to SPIP source code
Community support via forums and mailing lists

enrichment.short_description: Spline is a browser-based 3D design tool that lets designers and developers create, animate, and publish interactive 3D scenes for the web without a traditional 3D pipeline.
enrichment.seo_title: Spline: Browser-Based 3D Design and Animation Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Spline is a real-time 3D design tool for interactive scenes, product visuals, and web animation. Compare pricing plans, features, pros, and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Spline is a browser-based 3D design and animation platform founded in 2020 by Alejandro Leon, Andrei Linkov, and Vadim Konstantinov, and backed by Y Combinator. It lets designers, developers, and marketing teams create interactive 3D scenes, product visuals, and web experiences without needing a traditional 3D software pipeline like Blender or Cinema 4D. Spline runs entirely in the browser, supports real-time multiplayer collaboration similar to Figma, and can export projects as code (including React Three Fiber components), images, video, or embeddable web widgets. It also offers an AI add-on for text-to-3D and image-to-3D generation, plus AI texture and style transfer tools. Pricing follows a freemium model: a free plan supports limited files with watermarked web exports, a Starter plan starts at $12 per month billed annually (or $15 monthly) removing watermarks and unlocking unlimited files, a Professional plan is $20 per month billed annually (or $25 monthly) adding code export and unlimited scenes, and an Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with SSO, self-hosted exports, and dedicated support. Spline has raised over $41 million in funding, including a 2024 Series A, and is used by design and marketing teams to build interactive 3D landing pages, product configurators, and animated web content.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Spline
paragraphs: Spline is a real-time, browser-based 3D design tool built to make interactive 3D content as accessible as 2D design tools like Figma. Instead of requiring a full desktop 3D application and rendering pipeline, Spline lets teams model, texture, animate, and publish 3D scenes directly from a web browser.

Founded in 2020 and headquartered in San Francisco, Spline has grown into a widely used tool among product designers, marketing teams, and developers who want to add interactive 3D elements to websites, apps, and product pages without hiring specialist 3D artists for every project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Spline combines 3D modeling primitives, a material and texture library, and physics-based interactions in a single editor, so users can build objects that respond to clicks, scroll, or cursor movement. Its real-time multiplayer editing lets multiple designers work on the same scene simultaneously, with comments and version history similar to modern collaborative design tools.

Export options are a major part of Spline's workflow: scenes can be embedded directly into websites, exported as code components for React and other frameworks, or rendered as static images and video. The optional Spline AI add-on adds text-to-3D and image-to-3D generation, letting users create base 3D assets from prompts or reference images rather than modeling from scratch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Spline uses a per-seat freemium pricing model. The Free plan allows a limited number of files with unlimited viewers, but web exports carry a Spline watermark. The Starter plan, from $12 per month per editor billed annually, removes the watermark and unlocks unlimited files and folders.

The Professional plan, from $20 per month per editor billed annually, adds unlimited scenes per file, code export, and API and webhook access, while Enterprise pricing is custom and includes SAML SSO, self-hosted exports, and priority support. The Spline AI add-on is available for an additional $5 per seat per month with a monthly AI credit allowance.

features.title: Real-time multiplayer editing
features.description: Multiple team members can design in the same 3D scene simultaneously, with comments and version history.

features.title: Browser-based 3D modeling
features.description: Model, texture, and animate 3D objects directly in the browser without installing desktop 3D software.

features.title: Interactive and physics-based scenes
features.description: Add click, scroll, and hover interactions and physics behaviors to 3D objects for interactive web experiences.

features.title: Code and web export
features.description: Export scenes as embeddable web widgets, React components, images, or video for use in any website or app.

features.title: AI 3D generation add-on
features.description: Generate 3D assets and textures from text or image prompts using the optional Spline AI add-on.

features.title: Material and asset libraries
features.description: Access built-in libraries of materials, audio, and templates to speed up scene building.

features.title: Framework and platform integrations
features.description: Works alongside tools like Framer and Webflow to bring interactive 3D into existing web projects.

pros: Runs entirely in the browser with no heavy desktop 3D software required
Real-time collaboration makes it easy for design teams to work together
Flexible export options including code, video, image, and embeddable widgets
Generous free plan for exploring 3D design before upgrading
AI-assisted 3D and texture generation speeds up asset creation
cons: Free plan exports carry a visible watermark
Advanced features like code export and APIs require the Professional tier or higher
Steeper learning curve for users with no prior 3D design experience
Complex scenes can be demanding on browser performance
Enterprise features like SSO require custom-priced plans

faqs.question: Is Spline free to use
faqs.answer: Yes. Spline offers a free plan with limited files and watermarked web exports, with paid plans starting at $12 per month per editor for unlimited files and no watermark.

faqs.question: Do I need 3D design experience to use Spline
faqs.answer: No prior 3D experience is required, though there is a learning curve. Spline is designed to be more approachable than traditional 3D software like Blender or Cinema 4D.

faqs.question: Can Spline scenes be embedded on a website
faqs.answer: Yes. Spline scenes can be embedded directly into websites, exported as code components, or rendered as static images and video.

faqs.question: Does Spline support real-time collaboration
faqs.answer: Yes. Spline supports real-time multiplayer editing, similar to collaborative 2D design tools, so multiple people can work on a scene at once.

faqs.question: What is the Spline AI add-on
faqs.answer: Spline AI is an optional add-on, priced at $5 per seat per month, that generates 3D models and textures from text or image prompts using AI.

faqs.question: Who founded Spline
faqs.answer: Spline was founded in 2020 by Alejandro Leon, Andrei Linkov, and Vadim Konstantinov and is backed by Y Combinator.

faqs.question: What can Spline be used for
faqs.answer: Spline is commonly used to create interactive product visuals, animated landing pages, 3D web experiences, and design prototypes.

faqs.question: Does Spline offer an enterprise plan
faqs.answer: Yes. Spline Enterprise offers custom pricing with SAML SSO, self-hosted exports, version history, and priority support.

use_cases.title: Interactive marketing and product pages
use_cases.description: Marketing and design teams build animated 3D visuals and product showcases to increase engagement on landing pages.

use_cases.title: Product design prototyping
use_cases.description: Product designers create interactive 3D prototypes and UI concepts before handing them to engineering.

use_cases.title: Developer-integrated 3D web experiences
use_cases.description: Developers export Spline scenes as code components to embed interactive 3D directly into React and other web apps.

pricing_plans.price: $12/month per editor (billed annually) or $15/month billed monthly
$20/month per editor (billed annually) or $25/month billed monthly

enrichment.short_description: SpotDraft is an AI-native contract lifecycle management platform that helps legal, sales, and procurement teams draft, negotiate, sign, and manage contracts in one system.
enrichment.seo_title: SpotDraft: AI Contract Lifecycle Management Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SpotDraft is an AI-native CLM platform for drafting, negotiating, signing, and managing contracts. See features, pricing model, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SpotDraft is a contract lifecycle management (CLM) platform founded in 2017 by Shashank Bijapur and Madhav Bhagat, headquartered in Bengaluru, India with an additional office in New York. The platform helps in-house legal, sales, procurement, and HR teams manage the full contract lifecycle, from templated drafting and AI-assisted redlining through e-signature, approval workflows, and a searchable contract repository. SpotDraft markets itself as an AI-native CLM, positioning its automation around faster contract turnaround, with the company citing benchmarks such as contracts closing twice as fast and roughly 65 percent lower costs for customers. Pricing is not published; SpotDraft uses a custom, quote-based model billed per active user or by contract volume, with implementation, integrations, and 24 by 7 support included at no extra charge. The company has raised approximately 82 million dollars in funding, including a 54 million dollar Series B in February 2025 led by Vertex Growth Fund, and has stated it is profitable. SpotDraft primarily serves small and mid-market businesses with in-house legal teams that want structured contract workflows without heavy enterprise complexity, though it also supports larger enterprise customers through custom plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is SpotDraft
paragraphs: SpotDraft is a contract lifecycle management platform designed to take contracts from first draft through signature and ongoing management inside a single system. It targets in-house legal teams, along with the sales and procurement teams that generate and request contracts, aiming to reduce the back-and-forth typically involved in contract review.

Founded in 2017 and based in Bengaluru, India, SpotDraft has positioned itself as an AI-native CLM, building AI-assisted drafting and review into its core workflow rather than adding it as a bolt-on feature. The company has raised over 80 million dollars in venture funding and reports being profitable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: SpotDraft centers on a contract repository with searchable metadata, template-based drafting, and an in-app editor for redlining and negotiation. Its AI features assist with reviewing incoming contracts, flagging risky clauses, and extracting key terms and dates for tracking obligations and renewals.

The platform also includes native e-signature, configurable approval workflows for routing contracts to the right stakeholders, and Clickthrough, a module for managing high-volume click-to-accept agreements such as terms of service. Integrations with tools like Salesforce and Slack connect contract workflows to existing sales and communication systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SpotDraft does not publish fixed pricing tiers on its website. Instead, it uses a custom quote-based model, with plans priced either per active user per month or year, or based on contract volume for organizations with variable or seasonal usage.

Implementation, workflow setup, integrations, and support are included in the quoted price with no separate add-on fees. Third-party pricing guides estimate typical annual contracts starting in the range of ten thousand dollars and scaling with company size and feature needs, though exact figures require a sales conversation with SpotDraft.

features.title: AI-assisted contract review
features.description: Flags risky clauses and extracts key terms automatically to speed up contract review and negotiation.

features.title: Template-based contract drafting
features.description: Standardized templates and an in-app editor help teams generate consistent contracts quickly.

features.title: Native e-signature
features.description: Built-in digital signature capability removes the need for a separate e-signature tool.

features.title: Configurable approval workflows
features.description: Route contracts through custom approval chains based on contract type, value, or department.

features.title: Searchable contract repository
features.description: Centralized, searchable storage for all executed and in-progress contracts with metadata tracking.

features.title: Clickthrough agreements
features.description: A dedicated module for managing high-volume click-to-accept agreements like terms of service.

features.title: Obligation and renewal tracking
features.description: Automatically extracts dates and obligations to alert teams ahead of renewals or expirations.

features.title: CRM and workflow integrations
features.description: Connects with tools such as Salesforce and Slack to embed contract workflows into existing systems.

pros: AI-assisted review can meaningfully cut contract turnaround time
All-in-one platform covering drafting, signature, and repository
Implementation and support included without extra fees
Flexible pricing based on users or contract volume
Backed by significant funding and reports of profitability
cons: No public pricing, requiring a sales conversation to get a quote
No free trial or self-serve evaluation option
Custom quotes can make budgeting harder for smaller teams
Primarily suited to companies with an in-house legal function
Full feature depth may be more than very small teams need

faqs.question: What is SpotDraft used for
faqs.answer: SpotDraft is used for contract lifecycle management, covering drafting, negotiation, e-signature, approval workflows, and ongoing contract tracking in one platform.

faqs.question: How much does SpotDraft cost
faqs.answer: SpotDraft does not publish fixed pricing. It uses a custom quote-based model priced per user or by contract volume, with third-party estimates suggesting annual contracts often start around ten thousand dollars.

faqs.question: Where is SpotDraft headquartered
faqs.answer: SpotDraft is headquartered in Bengaluru, India, with an additional office in New York.

faqs.question: Does SpotDraft offer a free trial
faqs.answer: No. SpotDraft does not offer a public free trial or self-serve signup; prospective customers go through a sales demo process.

faqs.question: Does SpotDraft include e-signature
faqs.answer: Yes. SpotDraft includes a native e-signature capability so contracts can be signed without a separate tool.

faqs.question: Who founded SpotDraft
faqs.answer: SpotDraft was founded in 2017 by Shashank Bijapur and Madhav Bhagat.

faqs.question: What integrations does SpotDraft support
faqs.answer: SpotDraft integrates with tools such as Salesforce and Slack to connect contract workflows with existing sales and communication systems.

faqs.question: Who is SpotDraft best suited for
faqs.answer: SpotDraft is best suited for small and mid-market businesses with in-house legal teams, though it also supports larger enterprise customers.

use_cases.title: In-house legal contract management

use_cases.audience: In-house legal and general counsel teams

use_cases.title: Sales contract acceleration

use_cases.title: High-volume clickthrough agreements

use_cases.audience: Product and compliance teams managing online agreements

features: Priced per active user or by contract volume

enrichment.short_description: SpotMe is an enterprise event management platform used by life sciences, professional services, and technology companies to run virtual, hybrid, and in-person events.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SpotMe is an enterprise event platform for virtual, hybrid, and in-person events, built for life sciences and professional services. See plans and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SpotMe is an enterprise event management platform founded in 2000 as a spin-off from EPFL in Lausanne, Switzerland, where it remains headquartered today, with additional offices in Chicago, Sofia, and Singapore. Originally a hardware networking device company, SpotMe transformed into a SaaS event app platform in 2011 and has since focused heavily on enterprise customers in regulated industries such as life sciences, alongside professional services and technology companies. In April 2023, Swiss Post acquired a majority stake in SpotMe while the company continued to operate independently. The platform supports virtual, hybrid, and in-person events through a single system, offering more than 80 engagement features including streaming, breakout sessions, a studio for speakers, polls, gamification, and networking tools. A dedicated offering, Onomi, extends SpotMe's event technology for life sciences customer engagement and compliance needs. Pricing is quote-based across four tiers, Starter, Business, Advanced, and Enterprise, distinguished mainly by the number of events included, integration availability, and app branding options; SpotMe does not publish fixed prices and pricing does not scale with the number of event participants. SpotMe has been recognized as an event technology leader by G2 and Forrester.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is SpotMe
paragraphs: SpotMe is an enterprise event management platform that supports virtual, hybrid, and in-person events from a single system. Originally founded in 2000 as an EPFL spin-off in Lausanne, Switzerland, the company began as a hardware networking device maker before transforming into a SaaS event app platform in 2011.

SpotMe has built a particular focus on regulated industries, especially life sciences, where compliant engagement with healthcare professionals is a major requirement, alongside professional services and technology companies running large internal or customer-facing events.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform offers more than 80 engagement features spanning event apps for desktop, iOS, and Android, live streaming and broadcast studio tools for speakers, breakout sessions, polling, Q and A, and gamification. Its unlimited participants and admins model means pricing is not tied to attendee counts.

For enterprise customers, SpotMe adds integrations with marketing and event tools such as Marketo and Cvent, branded app store and Google Play apps, and single sign-on. Its Onomi offering extends the platform specifically for life sciences customer engagement, addressing compliance needs around interactions with healthcare professionals.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SpotMe does not publish fixed prices; all four plans, Starter, Business, Advanced, and Enterprise, require contacting sales for a custom quote. Plans are primarily differentiated by the number of events included per year, ranging from up to 2 events on Starter to unlimited events on Enterprise.

A notable aspect of SpotMe's pricing is that it does not increase based on the number of event participants, so organizations can scale attendance without cost surprises. Production add-on services, such as onsite coordination and live production support, are priced separately and start in the range of 3,000 to 7,700 dollars or more per engagement.

features.title: Unified virtual, hybrid, and in-person events
features.description: Run any event format from a single platform rather than switching tools by event type.

features.title: 80+ engagement features
features.description: Includes polls, Q and A, gamification, networking, and breakout sessions to drive attendee engagement.

features.title: Life sciences compliance support via Onomi

features.title: Marketing and CRM integrations
features.description: Connects with tools like Marketo and Cvent on higher-tier plans for lead and registration data flow.

features.title: Branded native apps
features.description: Enterprise customers can publish their own branded app in the App Store and Google Play.

features.title: 24/7 customer support
features.description: SpotMe offers round-the-clock support with a stated 30-second response target across plans.

Strong focus and compliance features for regulated life sciences events

Long track record dating back to 2000 with enterprise customer base
Backed by Swiss Post since its 2023 majority stake investment
cons: No public pricing, requiring a sales conversation for every tier
Number of events per year is capped on lower-tier plans
Advanced integrations and branded apps are limited to higher tiers
Production add-on services carry significant additional cost
May be more platform than smaller organizations running occasional events need

faqs.question: What is SpotMe used for
faqs.answer: SpotMe is used to plan and run virtual, hybrid, and in-person enterprise events, including conferences, internal meetings, and life sciences customer engagement events.

faqs.question: How much does SpotMe cost
faqs.answer: SpotMe does not publish fixed pricing. It offers four tiers, Starter, Business, Advanced, and Enterprise, all requiring a custom quote from sales.

faqs.question: Where is SpotMe headquartered
faqs.answer: SpotMe is headquartered in Lausanne, Switzerland, with additional offices in Chicago, Sofia, and Singapore.

faqs.question: When was SpotMe founded
faqs.answer: SpotMe was founded in 2000 as a spin-off from EPFL in Lausanne, Switzerland.

faqs.question: Does SpotMe pricing depend on attendee numbers
faqs.answer: No. SpotMe states its pricing does not change based on the number of participants in an event.

faqs.question: What is Onomi
faqs.answer: Onomi is SpotMe's event-centric customer engagement offering built specifically for the life sciences industry and its compliance requirements.

faqs.question: Who owns SpotMe
faqs.answer: Swiss Post acquired a majority stake in SpotMe in April 2023, while SpotMe continues to operate as an independent company.

faqs.question: Does SpotMe support hybrid events
faqs.answer: Yes. SpotMe supports virtual, hybrid, and in-person events from the same platform, including streaming and in-room engagement tools.

use_cases.title: Life sciences customer engagement events
use_cases.description: Pharmaceutical and life sciences companies use SpotMe and its Onomi offering to run compliant engagement events with healthcare professionals.

use_cases.title: Enterprise conferences and internal events

use_cases.audience: Enterprise event and internal communications teams

use_cases.title: Professional services client events
use_cases.description: Consulting and professional services firms use SpotMe to host client-facing events with branded, secure event apps.

Branded App Store and Google Play app

enrichment.short_description: SpotOn is a restaurant and small-business technology company offering point-of-sale systems, payment processing, online ordering, and business management tools.
enrichment.seo_title: SpotOn: Restaurant POS and Payments Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SpotOn provides restaurant POS, payment processing, online ordering, and management tools. Compare pricing model, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SpotOn is a restaurant and small-business technology company founded in 2017 by Doron Friedman, Matt Hyman, and Zach Hyman, headquartered in San Francisco, California. The company builds an integrated ecosystem of point-of-sale hardware and software, payment processing, online ordering and delivery, reservation and waitlist management, labor scheduling, payroll integration, marketing and loyalty tools, and AI-powered profitability analysis, aimed primarily at restaurants across casual dining, fine dining, bars, breweries, quick-service, and cafes. It also offers SpotOn Terminal, a payments and POS solution for other small businesses such as retail, salons, and automotive services. SpotOn is privately held with backing from major investors including Dragoneer Investment Group, Andreessen Horowitz, 01 Advisors, DST Global, and Franklin Templeton, and has grown to more than 2,600 employees. SpotOn markets its POS software plans as starting at 0 dollars per month with competitive processing rates, though most restaurants use its Core Bundle package with pricing that varies by business needs and typically requires a custom quote or demo. The company has been ranked the number one restaurant POS by G2 for multiple consecutive quarters.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is SpotOn
paragraphs: SpotOn is a restaurant and small-business technology company that combines point-of-sale hardware, payment processing, and back-office management software into a single ecosystem. It was founded in 2017 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

While SpotOn's primary focus is foodservice, spanning casual dining, fine dining, bars, breweries, quick-service, and cafes, it also serves general small businesses such as retail stores, salons, and automotive shops through SpotOn Terminal, a standalone POS and payments product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: SpotOn's restaurant platform includes cloud-based POS software, countertop terminals, handheld ordering devices, and kitchen display systems, alongside online ordering and delivery integration. Operational tools cover reservation and waitlist management, labor scheduling, and payroll integration, helping restaurants manage both the front and back of house.

On top of transaction processing, SpotOn provides marketing and loyalty programs to help restaurants drive repeat business, plus AI-powered profitability analysis that surfaces menu and cost insights. The platform has been recognized as the number one restaurant POS by G2 for six consecutive quarters.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SpotOn advertises its POS software plans as starting as low as 0 dollars per month, paired with competitive payment processing rates rather than a flat software fee. Most restaurants adopt SpotOn's Core Bundle package, which combines hardware, software, and payment processing.

Exact software and hardware pricing is not published in detail and typically requires requesting a demo or quote from SpotOn's sales team, since costs vary based on business type, transaction volume, and the specific hardware and add-on modules selected.

features.title: Cloud-based POS software
features.description: Point-of-sale software for restaurants that runs on countertop terminals and handheld devices.

features.title: Integrated payment processing
features.description: Built-in payment processing with competitive rates paired directly with the POS system.

features.title: Online ordering and delivery
features.description: Online ordering platform that integrates with the POS for a unified order flow.

features.title: Reservation and waitlist management
features.description: Tools for managing table reservations and walk-in waitlists in one system.

features.title: Labor scheduling and payroll integration
features.description: Staff scheduling tools that connect with payroll to streamline restaurant operations.

features.title: AI-powered profitability insights
features.description: Analyzes sales and cost data to surface menu and profitability insights for operators.

features.title: Marketing and loyalty programs
features.description: Built-in tools to run loyalty programs and marketing campaigns to drive repeat customers.

features.title: SpotOn Terminal for small businesses
features.description: A standalone POS and payments product for non-restaurant small businesses like retail and salons.

pros: Software plans can start at 0 dollars per month
All-in-one ecosystem covering POS, payments, ordering, and operations
Ranked number one restaurant POS by G2 for multiple consecutive quarters
Serves both restaurants and general small businesses via SpotOn Terminal
Backed by major investors, indicating financial stability
cons: Detailed hardware and software pricing is not published upfront
Requires a demo or sales conversation to get an accurate quote
Payment processing rates add to the total cost beyond the software fee
Primarily optimized for foodservice, with fewer industry-specific features outside restaurants
Full feature set may be more than very small or single-location operators need

faqs.question: What is SpotOn used for
faqs.answer: SpotOn is used by restaurants and small businesses for point-of-sale operations, payment processing, online ordering, reservations, labor scheduling, and marketing.

faqs.question: How much does SpotOn cost
faqs.answer: SpotOn advertises POS software starting as low as 0 dollars per month plus payment processing rates, though most restaurants use the Core Bundle package with pricing that requires a custom quote.

faqs.question: Where is SpotOn headquartered
faqs.answer: SpotOn is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: When was SpotOn founded
faqs.answer: SpotOn was founded in 2017 by Doron Friedman, Matt Hyman, and Zach Hyman.

faqs.question: Does SpotOn work for businesses other than restaurants
faqs.answer: Yes. SpotOn Terminal serves general small businesses such as retail stores, salons, and automotive shops, in addition to its core restaurant products.

faqs.question: Does SpotOn include online ordering
faqs.answer: Yes. SpotOn offers an online ordering and delivery platform that integrates directly with its POS system.

faqs.question: Is SpotOn a public or private company
faqs.answer: SpotOn is privately held, backed by investors including Dragoneer Investment Group, Andreessen Horowitz, 01 Advisors, DST Global, and Franklin Templeton.

faqs.question: Does SpotOn offer payroll features
faqs.answer: Yes. SpotOn includes labor scheduling with payroll integration as part of its restaurant management tools.

use_cases.title: Full-service restaurant operations
use_cases.description: Casual and fine dining restaurants use SpotOn for tableside POS, reservations, and staff scheduling in one system.

use_cases.title: Quick-service and cafe ordering
use_cases.description: Quick-service restaurants and cafes use SpotOn for fast checkout, online ordering, and kitchen display coordination.

use_cases.title: General small business payments
use_cases.description: Retail, salon, and automotive businesses use SpotOn Terminal for POS and payment processing outside foodservice.

features: Custom hardware and add-on pricing via quote

enrichment.short_description: Spreaker is a podcast hosting and monetization platform, owned by iHeartMedia, that lets creators publish, distribute, track, and monetize podcasts from one dashboard.
enrichment.seo_title: Spreaker: Podcast Hosting and Monetization Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Spreaker is a podcast hosting platform owned by iHeartMedia offering distribution, analytics, and monetization. Compare plans, features, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Spreaker is a podcast hosting and monetization platform founded in 2010 by Francesco Baschieri. In 2018, Spreaker merged with BlogTalkRadio to form Voxnest, and in 2020 iHeartMedia acquired Voxnest, bringing Spreaker under the iHeartMedia umbrella. The platform lets podcasters manage their RSS feed, automatically distribute episodes to major platforms like Spotify and Apple Podcasts, track listener analytics, and monetize shows through built-in advertising, subscriptions, and a Supporters Club membership feature. Spreaker offers a free-forever plan, Free Speech, that includes unlimited episodes and automatic ad monetization but takes a 20 percent fee on Supporters Club revenue and hosts only one podcast with 6 months of statistics. Paid plans scale up from Broadcaster at 20 euros per month, which adds unlimited podcasts and removes the Supporters Club fee, to Anchorman at 50 euros per month with full collaboration and statistics, up to Publisher at 250 euros per month, which adds an ad campaign manager for direct ad sales and IAB category blocking, aimed at professional broadcasters and media companies. Because of its iHeartMedia ownership, Spreaker also offers enterprise-level distribution and monetization infrastructure for high-volume podcast networks.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Spreaker
paragraphs: Spreaker is a podcast hosting platform that lets creators publish, distribute, and monetize podcasts from a single dashboard. It was founded in 2010 by Francesco Baschieri and later merged with BlogTalkRadio in 2018 to form Voxnest, which iHeartMedia acquired in 2020.

Today Spreaker operates as part of iHeartMedia's podcast infrastructure, serving both independent podcasters looking for simple, reliable hosting and larger broadcasters and media companies that need enterprise-scale distribution and ad monetization tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Spreaker manages a show's RSS feed and automatically distributes episodes to major listening platforms, while providing listener analytics and a customizable embeddable player. Monetization tools include automatic ad insertion, subscription support, and a Supporters Club feature for direct listener support.

Higher-tier plans add collaboration features for teams producing a show together, extended historical statistics, and, on the Publisher plan, an ad campaign manager for selling ads directly along with IAB category blocking to control which ad categories appear on a show.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Spreaker's Free Speech plan is free forever and includes unlimited episodes, automatic ad monetization, and 6 months of statistics, but hosts only one podcast and takes a 20 percent fee on Supporters Club revenue.

Paid plans start at the Broadcaster tier for 20 euros per month, offering unlimited podcasts and 12 months of statistics with no Supporters Club fee, scaling up through Anchorman at 50 euros per month for unlimited collaborators and full statistics, to Publisher at 250 euros per month for professional broadcasters needing direct ad sales tools and 24 months of statistics.

features.title: RSS feed hosting and management
features.description: Manage and customize a podcast's RSS feed directly within Spreaker's dashboard.

features.title: Automatic multi-platform distribution
features.description: Episodes are automatically distributed to major platforms including Spotify and Apple Podcasts.

features.title: Built-in ad monetization
features.description: Automatic ad insertion lets creators earn revenue without arranging sponsorships manually.

features.title: Supporters Club subscriptions
features.description: A membership feature that lets listeners directly support a show with recurring payments.

features.title: Listener analytics and statistics
features.description: Tracks listener numbers and engagement, with historical statistics ranging from 6 to 24 months depending on plan.

features.title: Live broadcasting tools
features.description: Supports live streaming and broadcasting in addition to standard episode publishing.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Higher-tier plans support unlimited collaborators for teams producing a show together.

features.title: Direct ad sales tools
features.description: The Publisher plan includes an ad campaign manager and IAB category blocking for professional advertisers.

pros: Generous free-forever plan with unlimited episodes
Backed by iHeartMedia, providing scale and monetization infrastructure
Built-in monetization tools including ads and listener subscriptions
Simple, dashboard-based workflow for publishing and distribution
Scales from individual creators up to professional broadcasters
cons: Free plan limits hosting to a single podcast
Free plan takes a 20 percent cut of Supporters Club revenue
Higher-tier plans needed for extended statistics history and collaboration
Pricing is listed in euros, which may complicate budgeting for some regions
Top-tier Publisher plan is priced well above typical independent creator budgets

faqs.question: Is Spreaker free
faqs.answer: Yes. Spreaker offers a free-forever Free Speech plan with unlimited episodes and automatic ad monetization, though it hosts only one podcast.

faqs.question: Who owns Spreaker
faqs.answer: Spreaker is owned by iHeartMedia, which acquired its parent company Voxnest, formed from the 2018 merger of Spreaker and BlogTalkRadio, in 2020.

faqs.question: When was Spreaker founded
faqs.answer: Spreaker was founded in 2010 by Francesco Baschieri.

faqs.question: How does Spreaker monetization work
faqs.answer: Spreaker supports monetization through automatic ad insertion, listener subscriptions, and a Supporters Club membership feature, with direct ad sales tools on the Publisher plan.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest paid Spreaker plan
faqs.answer: The Broadcaster plan is the cheapest paid tier at 20 euros per month, offering unlimited podcasts and removing the Supporters Club fee.

faqs.question: Does Spreaker support live broadcasting
faqs.answer: Yes. Spreaker supports live streaming and broadcasting in addition to standard episode publishing and distribution.

faqs.question: Can I upgrade my Spreaker plan later
faqs.answer: Yes. Spreaker allows users to start on a lower plan and upgrade at any time, with prorated billing.

faqs.question: Who is Spreaker best suited for
faqs.answer: Spreaker suits independent podcasters wanting simple free hosting, as well as professional broadcasters and media companies needing advanced monetization and ad sales tools.

use_cases.title: Independent podcast creators
use_cases.description: Individual creators use the free plan to publish, distribute, and monetize a podcast with no upfront cost.

use_cases.title: Professional broadcasters and media companies
use_cases.description: Broadcasters use higher-tier plans for direct ad sales, extended analytics, and team collaboration on shows.

use_cases.title: Live radio and streaming shows
use_cases.description: Hosts use Spreaker's live broadcasting tools to stream radio-style shows alongside standard podcast episodes.
use_cases.audience: Live radio and streaming show hosts

features: Ad campaign manager for direct ad sales

enrichment.short_description: Spree Commerce is an open-source, headless e-commerce platform built on Ruby on Rails, offering a free self-hosted Community Edition and a paid Enterprise Edition for B2B and marketplace commerce.
enrichment.seo_title: Spree Commerce: Open-Source Headless Ecommerce Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Spree Commerce is a free, open-source headless ecommerce platform on Ruby on Rails, with a paid Enterprise Edition for B2B and marketplace commerce.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Spree Commerce is an open-source, headless e-commerce platform built on Ruby on Rails, created by Sean Schofield in 2007. Its Community Edition is free and self-hosted under a BSD-3-Clause open-source license, providing a full storefront, product catalog, checkout, and REST APIs for direct-to-consumer stores, and it has surpassed 15,000 GitHub stars and 2.7 million RubyGems downloads. Spree was acquired by First Data in 2015 to expand into open-source commerce and payments technology, and the project has since continued development with a broad open-source contributor base of over 800 contributors; today, a team operating as Vendo maintains and develops Spree Commerce. Companies that have used Spree include Goop, Craftsman, Kenmore, Blue Bottle Coffee, Fortnum and Mason, and GoDaddy. Alongside the free Community Edition, Spree offers a commercially licensed Enterprise Edition with custom pricing that adds B2B commerce, multi-vendor marketplace, and multi-tenant white-label SaaS modules, plus a dedicated success manager, SLA-backed support, and enterprise security features like SSO and audit logging. Spree is aimed at developers and growing DTC brands that want full ownership of their commerce stack without vendor lock-in or recurring platform fees, and it supports multi-region, multi-currency, and multi-language stores out of the box.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Spree Commerce
paragraphs: Spree Commerce is an open-source, headless e-commerce platform built on Ruby on Rails, created by Sean Schofield in 2007. It gives developers a fully self-hostable commerce engine with no vendor lock-in and no mandatory platform fees, since the core Community Edition is licensed under BSD-3-Clause.

The project has grown through more than 800 contributors and has been used by well-known brands such as Goop, Blue Bottle Coffee, and Fortnum and Mason. Following its 2015 acquisition by First Data, the project has continued as an actively maintained open-source platform, with a team operating as Vendo now driving its development and offering a commercial Enterprise Edition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Spree provides a complete commerce engine out of the box: product catalog, storefront, checkout, and both storefront and admin REST APIs, along with a TypeScript SDK and a production-ready Next.js storefront for teams that want a modern headless front end. It natively supports multi-region, multi-currency, and multi-language stores.

Because it is self-hosted, teams own their full stack and data, and can customize checkout flows, catalogs, and integrations without waiting on a vendor roadmap. The paid Enterprise Edition builds on this foundation with B2B commerce, multi-vendor marketplace, and multi-tenant white-label SaaS modules for companies that need those capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Spree Commerce Community Edition is completely free and open source under the BSD-3-Clause license, with no fees for self-hosting, unlimited stores, or unlimited transactions since there is no vendor billing relationship for the core software.

The Enterprise Edition is commercially licensed with custom pricing available on request, adding paid add-on modules for B2B, multi-vendor marketplace, and multi-tenant white-label SaaS, along with dedicated support, SLA-backed response times, and enterprise security features such as SSO and audit logging.

features.title: Headless architecture with REST APIs
features.description: Storefront and admin REST APIs let developers build custom front ends on any framework.

features.title: Next.js storefront and TypeScript SDK
features.description: A production-ready Next.js storefront and TypeScript SDK speed up building a modern headless front end.

features.title: Self-hosted, no vendor lock-in
features.description: Fully self-hostable with no mandatory platform fees or dependency on a single vendor's infrastructure.

features.title: Multi-region and multi-currency support
features.description: Native support for multiple regions, currencies, and languages within a single store setup.

features.title: Sales channels and order routing
features.description: Configure per-channel catalogs and rules-based order routing across sales channels.

features.title: Stock reservations
features.description: Built-in stock reservation logic helps manage inventory accurately across orders and channels.

features.title: B2B and marketplace modules (Enterprise)
features.description: Paid add-on modules extend Spree with B2B commerce and multi-vendor marketplace capabilities.

features.title: Enterprise security and support
features.description: Enterprise Edition adds SSO, audit logging, role-based access control, and SLA-backed support.

pros: Community Edition is completely free and open source with no platform fees
Full ownership of the commerce stack with no vendor lock-in
Built on Ruby on Rails with a mature, long-standing codebase since 2007
Modern headless architecture with REST APIs and a Next.js storefront
Large open-source contributor base and proven use by major brands
cons: Self-hosting requires developer resources and Ruby on Rails expertise
Community Edition is limited to direct-to-consumer use, not B2B or marketplace
Enterprise Edition pricing is not published and requires a custom quote
No official managed hosting from Spree itself, requiring third-party infrastructure
Smaller ecosystem compared to hosted platforms like Shopify

faqs.question: Is Spree Commerce free
faqs.answer: Yes. The Spree Commerce Community Edition is free and open source under the BSD-3-Clause license, available for self-hosting at no cost.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Spree Community and Enterprise Edition
faqs.answer: Community Edition is a free, self-hosted, direct-to-consumer commerce engine, while Enterprise Edition is commercially licensed and adds B2B, multi-vendor marketplace, and multi-tenant modules plus dedicated support.

faqs.question: What technology is Spree Commerce built on
faqs.answer: Spree Commerce is built on Ruby on Rails, with REST APIs, a TypeScript SDK, and a production-ready Next.js storefront available for headless front ends.

faqs.question: Who created Spree Commerce
faqs.answer: Spree Commerce was created by Sean Schofield in 2007 and has since grown through contributions from over 800 developers.

faqs.question: Does Spree Commerce support multiple currencies and languages
faqs.answer: Yes. Spree natively supports multi-region, multi-currency, and multi-language store configurations.

faqs.question: Is Spree Commerce suitable for B2B ecommerce
faqs.answer: The free Community Edition is direct-to-consumer only; B2B commerce capabilities are available through the paid Enterprise Edition.

faqs.question: Who maintains Spree Commerce today
faqs.answer: Spree Commerce is currently maintained and developed by a team operating as Vendo, following its 2015 acquisition by First Data.

faqs.question: What companies use Spree Commerce
faqs.answer: Companies that have used Spree Commerce include Goop, Blue Bottle Coffee, Fortnum and Mason, Craftsman, Kenmore, and GoDaddy.

use_cases.title: Developer-built custom storefronts
use_cases.description: Development teams use Spree's REST APIs and Next.js storefront to build fully custom, headless shopping experiences.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted DTC brands
use_cases.description: Growing direct-to-consumer brands use the free Community Edition to avoid recurring platform fees while owning their stack.

use_cases.title: B2B and marketplace commerce
use_cases.description: Enterprises needing multi-vendor marketplace or B2B ordering use the paid Enterprise Edition modules on top of Spree's core.

features: SSO, audit logging, and role-based access control

enrichment.short_description: Sprinto is a compliance automation platform that helps SaaS and technology companies achieve and maintain certifications like SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and HIPAA through continuous monitoring.
enrichment.seo_title: Sprinto: Automated SOC 2 and ISO 27001 Compliance
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sprinto automates SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and HIPAA compliance with continuous monitoring and evidence collection. See features, pricing, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sprinto is a compliance automation platform founded in 2020 by Girish Redekar and Raghuveer Kancherla, headquartered in Bangalore, India, with a growing presence in San Francisco and offices across multiple continents. The platform connects to a company's cloud infrastructure, identity providers, HR systems, and code repositories through APIs, then runs automated tests against frameworks such as SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and HIPAA, covering more than 200 frameworks and standards in total. It continuously collects timestamped evidence and surfaces a real-time compliance dashboard, reducing the manual screenshot-and-spreadsheet work traditionally required to prepare for security audits. Sprinto reports crossing 3,000 customers across 75 countries, supports more than 300 integrations, and launched an AI-driven compliance capability, Sprinto AI, in 2025. The company has raised venture funding including a 20 million dollar Series B round in April 2024. Pricing is not published on Sprinto's website and is quote-based, varying by company size and the number of frameworks pursued; third-party estimates suggest a single framework such as SOC 2 typically runs in the range of 6,000 to 10,000 dollars per year, with multi-framework or enterprise plans costing significantly more. Sprinto is aimed primarily at SaaS and technology companies that need to pass customer security reviews and maintain ongoing compliance without a large dedicated compliance team.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Sprinto
paragraphs: Sprinto is a compliance automation platform that helps technology and SaaS companies achieve and maintain security certifications such as SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and HIPAA. Founded in 2020 by Girish Redekar and Raghuveer Kancherla, the company is headquartered in Bangalore, India, with a growing footprint in the United States.

Rather than treating compliance as a one-time audit sprint, Sprinto is built around continuous monitoring, connecting directly to a company's cloud and business systems so that controls are checked and evidence is collected on an ongoing basis rather than manually before each audit.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Sprinto integrates with cloud infrastructure providers, identity and access management tools, HR systems, and code repositories through more than 300 integrations, running automated tests against the specific controls required by each compliance framework a company selects. It supports over 200 frameworks and standards beyond the core SOC 2 and ISO 27001 offerings.

The platform maintains a real-time compliance dashboard showing control status and outstanding gaps, along with a policy template library and risk register to support broader governance, risk, and compliance (GRC) needs. In 2025, Sprinto introduced Sprinto AI to further automate evidence review and compliance workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sprinto does not publish fixed pricing tiers; costs are quote-based and depend on company size, the number of frameworks pursued, and integration complexity. Third-party pricing guides estimate that a single framework, such as SOC 2 or ISO 27001, typically costs in the range of 6,000 to 10,000 dollars per year.

Pricing for organizations pursuing multiple frameworks in an Advanced tier is estimated at roughly 11,000 to 15,000 dollars per year by third-party sources, while Enterprise-level plans typically start around 20,000 dollars and can exceed 25,000 dollars depending on scope and region. Prospective customers should confirm exact pricing directly with Sprinto's sales team.

features.title: Automated evidence collection
features.description: Continuously collects timestamped evidence from connected systems instead of requiring manual screenshots before audits.

features.title: 200+ framework support
features.description: Supports SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, HIPAA, and more than 200 total frameworks and standards.

features.title: 300+ integrations
features.description: Connects to cloud infrastructure, identity providers, HR systems, and code repositories to monitor controls automatically.

features.title: Real-time compliance dashboard
features.description: Shows live control status and outstanding gaps rather than a static, point-in-time audit snapshot.

features.title: Risk register and vendor risk management
features.description: Tracks organizational and vendor risk alongside core compliance controls.

features.title: Policy template library
features.description: Provides ready-to-use policy templates to speed up building a compliance program from scratch.

features.title: Audit-ready reporting
features.description: Generates reports designed to streamline the external audit process with auditors.

features.title: Sprinto AI
features.description: An AI-driven capability, launched in 2025, that further automates evidence review and compliance workflows.

pros: Continuous, automated monitoring reduces manual audit-prep work
Broad framework coverage beyond just SOC 2 and ISO 27001
Large integration catalog covering common cloud and business tools
Real-time dashboard gives ongoing visibility into compliance status
Established customer base of 3,000-plus companies across 75 countries
cons: No public pricing, requiring a sales conversation for an exact quote
Costs can scale significantly for multi-framework or enterprise plans
Setup requires connecting multiple internal systems via integrations
Best suited to companies already using cloud infrastructure Sprinto can monitor
Third-party pricing estimates vary, so actual cost is only confirmed via quote

faqs.question: What is Sprinto used for
faqs.answer: Sprinto is used to automate compliance work for frameworks such as SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and HIPAA, including continuous control monitoring and audit evidence collection.

faqs.question: How much does Sprinto cost
faqs.answer: Sprinto pricing is quote-based and not published publicly; third-party estimates suggest a single framework typically costs 6,000 to 10,000 dollars per year, with multi-framework and enterprise plans costing more.

faqs.question: Where is Sprinto headquartered
faqs.answer: Sprinto is headquartered in Bangalore, India, with a growing presence in San Francisco and other international offices.

faqs.question: When was Sprinto founded
faqs.answer: Sprinto was founded in 2020 by Girish Redekar and Raghuveer Kancherla.

faqs.question: What frameworks does Sprinto support
faqs.answer: Sprinto supports SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and HIPAA, along with more than 200 total frameworks and standards.

faqs.question: How does Sprinto collect compliance evidence
faqs.answer: Sprinto connects to cloud infrastructure, identity providers, HR systems, and code repositories via API integrations to automatically and continuously collect timestamped evidence.

faqs.question: Does Sprinto help with audits
faqs.answer: Yes. Sprinto generates audit-ready reports and evidence to streamline the external audit process with auditors.

faqs.question: Who is Sprinto best suited for
faqs.answer: Sprinto is best suited for SaaS and technology companies that need to pass customer security reviews and maintain ongoing compliance without a large dedicated compliance team.

use_cases.title: First-time SOC 2 certification
use_cases.description: Early-stage SaaS startups use Sprinto to prepare for and pass their first SOC 2 audit ahead of enterprise sales cycles.

use_cases.title: Multi-framework compliance management
use_cases.description: Scaling companies use Sprinto to manage SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and other frameworks together from one dashboard.

use_cases.title: Continuous security monitoring
use_cases.description: Security and compliance teams use Sprinto for ongoing, automated control monitoring rather than periodic manual reviews.

pricing_plans.price: Custom pricing (third-party estimates range from approximately $6,000 to $25,000+ per year depending on frameworks and company size)
features: Framework selection including SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and HIPAA
